does: Themis introduces an integrated framework combining explainable AI (XAI) with reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). The system enables transparent training of RL agents while incorporating human preferences to align behavior with intended goals. It supports over 200 standard RL environments and includes configurable modules for experimentation. The framework demonstrates that reward models trained through human preference feedback can match or exceed performance of ground-truth reward signals. Themis also provides a cloud-based platform for collecting human feedback at scale, supporting automated experiment management and participant coordination across multiple concurrent studies, validated to handle approximately 1,000 concurrent users on standard commercial hardware.
connects: This work builds on the established literature combining transparency and safety in RL. It responds to growing concerns about RL agent misalignment by integrating explainability methods—a line of work emphasizing interpretability of agent decisions—with RLHF, which has emerged as a promising alignment technique following its success in large language model training. The paper addresses a gap identified in the safety literature: while explainability and human feedback each show independent promise, no unified framework previously combined both approaches systematically. It extends prior work on preference-based learning and human-in-the-loop RL by adding transparency mechanisms, and builds on infrastructure work in interactive machine learning platforms.
enables: This framework opens pathways for large-scale empirical studies on human-AI alignment, allowing researchers to systematically test how explainability affects human judgment quality and preference elicitation. It enables investigation of whether transparency genuinely improves human oversight or introduces new failure modes. The infrastructure supports future work on scaling human feedback collection and developing better methods for converting human preferences into robust reward functions. The publicly available nature of Themis could standardize evaluation practices across alignment research, facilitating comparison of different explainability and feedback-collection strategies. It also enables deployment scenarios where human-in-the-loop oversight is continuous, supporting real-time alignment monitoring during agent operation.
